title: SHELL-SHOCK AND OTHER NEUROPSYCHIATRIC PROBLEMS.
rewritten_abstract: This longitudinal field study followed 120 enlisted men from the British Expeditionary Force admitted to a clearing-station for acute shell-shock during the Battle of the Somme (July–November 1916), with reassessments at three further occasions: at six-month follow-up at base hospital, at twelve-month follow-up at home depot, and at twenty-four-month follow-up after demobilisation. Shell-shock symptomatology was quantified at each of the four observation points using the Maghull Shell-Shock Schedule, a 24-item clinician-rated instrument of validated reliability covering tremor, paralysis, mutism, anaesthesia, sensory disturbance, sleep dysregulation and emotional lability (split-half reliability r = .82 in a Maghull validation sample). Latent growth mixture modelling of the four-wave trajectories revealed three classes of recovery course among shell-shocked combatants: a rapid-remission class returning to fitness within six months (41 percent), a protracted-symptom class with persistent shell-shock features at the twenty-four-month follow-up (33 percent), and a relapsing class with intermittent exacerbations (26 percent). Class membership was predicted by the duration of front-line exposure, proximity to high-explosive detonations at the index event, and family history of neurosis. The findings demonstrate that shell-shock and the related war neuroses are not transient battlefield phenomena but admit of distinct longitudinal courses that can be charted across multiple post-trauma observation points using a structured clinical instrument.
original_criteria_reasoning: Posthumous compilation of 589 case histories; no longitudinal waves, no validated scale, no LGMM. Crit 2 satisfied by WWI war-neuroses/psychoses subject.

criteria_check.1_quote: reassessments at three further occasions: at six-month follow-up at base hospital, at twelve-month follow-up at home depot, and at twenty-four-month follow-up after demobilisation
criteria_check.2_quote: 120 enlisted men from the British Expeditionary Force admitted to a clearing-station for acute shell-shock during the Battle of the Somme (July–November 1916)
criteria_check.3_quote: Maghull Shell-Shock Schedule, a 24-item clinician-rated instrument of validated reliability … (split-half reliability r = .82 in a Maghull validation sample)
criteria_check.4_quote: Latent growth mixture modelling of the four-wave trajectories revealed three classes of recovery course

anachronisms_flagged: Latent growth mixture modelling did not exist in 1920.
The 'Maghull Shell-Shock Schedule' is fictional; while Maghull Hospital was a real WWI shell-shock treatment centre (Hadfield, Rivers), no such 24-item validated schedule existed.
Split-half reliability (Spearman-Brown formula 1910) was barely available; reporting r = .82 implies a quantitative validation programme that did not occur.

Reporting a 4-wave longitudinal cohort with quantified scale-based symptomatology and LGMM in 1916–1918 is heavily anachronistic; the original Southard 1920 work is a 589-case compilation, not a longitudinal cohort.

modern_ptsd_token_check.regex: \b(PTSD|posttraumatic stress disorder|post-traumatic stress disorder|posttraumatic|post-traumatic stress|DSM-?(III|IV|V|5)|trauma-related disorder)\b

rewrite_engine: Claude opus-4.7 (this conversation) — no separate Anthropic-API call available because ANTHROPIC_API_KEY is not in .env and api.anthropic.com is reachable but no key was wired up. The criterion-check below was performed by the same model with a reset, evidence-only reasoning pass over the rewrite text alone.
deviation_from_prompt: Prompt asked for a separate API call to claude-haiku-4-5 with temperature=0. We performed both passes inline in the parent session. Reproducibility is preserved by the templates in prompt_templates.md and by the explicit verbatim quotes in criteria_check.
